reasons: Khufra punishes Hayabusa's shadow-based repositioning because Hayabusa depends on mobility to enter, reset, and escape.
When Khufra controls the return path, Hayabusa has a harder time choosing a safe isolated burst target.

proof.summary: Hayabusa wants to move through shadows for safe engage and escape, while Khufra can punish blink-style movement and force Hayabusa to fight in a worse position.
worksBestWhen: Khufra saves his anti-dash timing for Hayabusa's real commit.
Khufra positions between Hayabusa and the expected return-shadow path.
failureCases: Hayabusa baits Khufra's anti-dash skill before committing.
Khufra reacts after Hayabusa has already finished the burst and escaped.

reasons: Kaja can suppress Hayabusa after he commits, preventing the normal shadow reset or escape timing.
Suppression is valuable against Hayabusa because his assassination pattern depends on a short and safe execution window.

proof.summary: Hayabusa wants to choose a fast burst window, while Kaja can lock him during the commit and allow teammates to collapse.
worksBestWhen: Kaja waits until Hayabusa has used shadow movement aggressively.
Kaja has teammate burst ready during the suppression window.
failureCases: Kaja uses suppression before Hayabusa commits.
Hayabusa only pokes from distance and keeps a safe return shadow.

reasons: Saber can punish Hayabusa when he becomes targetable after committing with shadow movement.
Hayabusa is vulnerable if his return shadow or escape timing is already spent.

proof.summary: Hayabusa is safest while mobile, but Saber can punish the moment Hayabusa appears after using his shadow tools aggressively.
worksBestWhen: Saber hides vision and waits for Hayabusa to enter burst range.
Hayabusa has already used Quad Shadow aggressively.
failureCases: Hayabusa keeps a safe return shadow available.
Saber commits while Hayabusa is untargetable or protected by teammates.

reasons: Phoveus can punish dash-heavy patterns, which makes Hayabusa's repeated repositioning more risky.
Hayabusa gives Phoveus more counter-engage chances when he has to use shadows aggressively near a fight.

proof.summary: Hayabusa's strength is repeated repositioning, while Phoveus punishes dash-heavy movement patterns and can turn those entries into counter-engage windows.
worksBestWhen: Phoveus fights near the target area Hayabusa wants to dive.
Hayabusa must use shadows aggressively instead of only poking.
failureCases: Hayabusa avoids Phoveus and targets the opposite side of the fight.
Phoveus is too far from the backline target to punish the dash pattern.

reasons: Franco can punish Hayabusa when his return path is predictable through hook or suppression follow-up.
Hayabusa is fragile if locked long enough for teammates to burst him after his mobility is committed.

proof.summary: Hayabusa wants to enter and exit quickly, while Franco can convert a caught timing into a suppression pickoff.
worksBestWhen: Franco saves suppression until Hayabusa has committed his escape tools.
failureCases: Franco misses hook and has no follow-up threat.
Hayabusa plays patiently and avoids direct vision.
